overall_assessment.one_sentence_verdict: Baseline fit is good for AI Architect — EEA because the curated store already proves enterprise architecture, governed AI workflow delivery, reliability, and stakeholder translation, but the current master-CV representation still undersells that fit and over-leans on generic leadership framing.
overall_assessment.uncertainty_note: This category is based on a sparse sample of 10 jobs and the representation layer is proxy-derived, so the assessment emphasizes repeated architecture, governance, reliability, and stakeholder patterns rather than niche EEA/vendor signals.
citations: Soft skills: communication 9/10/90; collaboration 7/10/70; stakeholder management 7/10/70.

weighted_score_explanation: Coverage is strong because curated evidence already spans brownfield modernization, greenfield architecture, governed AI workflows, observability, CI/CD, cost-aware delivery, and stakeholder translation across Seven.One and Daypaio.

Curation completeness is good but not complete because current-role architect-title provenance and stronger architect-facing role framing still sit upstream in knowledge-base records rather than in reusable metadata.

Representation quality is the weakest component because the current headline and summary in role metadata surface generic 'Engineering Leader / Software Architect' framing and brownfield AdTech modernization, while under-surfacing governed AI workflow delivery and player-coach architect scope.

AI architecture fit is high because the curated store supports enterprise platform design, AI workflow architecture, guardrails, structured outputs, access control, evaluation, and production reliability without relying on unsafe model-training or fine-tuning claims.

Leadership scope fit is good when framed as player-coach technical leadership, mentoring, architecture direction, and stakeholder translation; it drops if framed as formal people-management or executive ownership.

Impact proof is strong because multiple achievements carry measurable outcomes: 75% incident reduction, 3 years zero downtime, 2,000 users, 42 plugins, onboarding 6 to 3 months, velocity +25%, and release cycles from weeks to days.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Brownfield enterprise platform modernization with measurable reliability and cost outcomes
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: This category is architecture-led and expects architects who can redesign legacy enterprise systems while improving production delivery and reliability.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by legacy-to-event-driven transformation, architectural runway, and observability pipeline evidence in Seven.One; directly matches the category anchor of platform design in 10/10 and brownfield scope in 8/10.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Governed enterprise AI workflow platform delivery at internal scale
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: EEA-fit here depends more on governed agentic delivery than on model-training or fine-tuning claims.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by Commander-4/Joyia evidence covering 2,000 users, 42 plugins, guardrail profiles, per-silo access control, structured outputs, semantic caching, and evaluation harnesses; this aligns with agents_orchestration 6/10 and guardrails_governance 6/10.

strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The market heavily rewards architects who translate ambiguous business requirements into technical direction and delivery roadmaps.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by technical-debt ROI communication, business-metric realization, risk communication, and event-storming/domain discovery evidence; this maps well to communication 9/10, stakeholder management 7/10, and collaboration 7/10.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Production reliability, observability, and delivery mechanics
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Reliable production delivery separates architects who shipped governed systems from those who only proposed designs.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by observability pipeline, monitoring/incident management, CI/CD modernization, and production observability stack evidence; the category repeatedly values reliability (9) and delivery (9).

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Player-coach technical leadership without needing executive framing
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The sample favors IC/player-coach architect scope over heavy people-management or executive ownership.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by mentoring, DDD standards-setting, and north-star architecture direction; this fits the category’s IC/player-coach bias and avoids overclaiming hiring/performance ownership.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Greenfield 0→1 architecture foundation from a prior tech-lead role
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The category likes architects who can handle both greenfield and brownfield scope, not just modernization.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by event-sourced CRM platform launch, event-storming requirements shaping, CI/CD automation, and scalability architecture evidence at Daypaio; this strengthens the category’s greenfield-plus-brownfield pattern.

gap_analysis.signal: Architecture-first AI-architect headline and summary framing
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: This category wants architecture plus governed delivery at the top of the CV, and the current metadata does not surface that clearly.
gap_analysis.current_state: The current headline is 'Engineering Leader / Software Architect' and the summary foregrounds legacy modernization but omits the strongest governed AI workflow architecture evidence.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The curated store supports a player-coach software architect with enterprise AI workflow, governance, reliability, and stakeholder-translation evidence; it does not require executive framing.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Surface architect/player-coach scope plus enterprise AI workflow governance in the headline and summary, anchored in Commander-4 plus Seven.One modernization evidence.
gap_analysis.citation: Underrepresented in data/master-cv/role_metadata.json:3 despite curated support for governed AI workflow delivery and architecture in Seven.One and Commander-4; category headline guidance explicitly prefers architecture-first, delivery-backed framing.

gap_analysis.why_it_matters: Safe architect title family is easier to support when canonical metadata contains provenance-backed architect wording for the current role.
gap_analysis.current_state: Upstream knowledge-base records repeatedly label the current role as 'Lead Software Engineer / Technical Architect' or 'Technical Lead - Addressable TV', but canonical role metadata still exposes only 'Technical Lead (Addressable TV)'.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: A technical-architect descriptor appears well supported upstream, but it is not yet downstream-safe until promoted into curated metadata with provenance.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Promote the architect-title variant into data/master-cv/role_metadata.json and current-role metadata, preserving provenance to knowledge-base records.
gap_analysis.citation: Not yet curated from multiple current-role STAR records in docs/archive/knowledge-base.md into canonical metadata; this directly affects safe use of the 'Solutions Architect / AI Architect' title family.

gap_analysis.signal: Greenfield 0→1 platform architecture and roadmap shaping
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The sample explicitly rewards architects who can handle greenfield plus brownfield scope, and this candidate has both.
gap_analysis.current_state: Daypaio contains strong greenfield architecture, event-storming, CI/CD, and scalability evidence, but it is not surfaced in the current role-priority representation or summary.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The candidate can safely claim prior greenfield platform architecture and roadmap translation experience without inflating it into enterprise-architect-wide scope.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Elevate Daypaio’s 0→1 platform, event-storming, and scalability bullets into the master-CV’s top supporting roles for this category.
gap_analysis.citation: Supported by curated Daypaio evidence but underrepresented in the current master-CV priority ordering; category guidance prefers greenfield plus brownfield coverage.

gap_analysis.signal: Governed AI delivery language is too fragmented across projects and skills
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: This category is differentiated by governed agentic delivery, not by narrow retrieval-tool keywords alone.
gap_analysis.current_state: Commander-4 and Lantern prove guardrails, structured outputs, evaluation, observability, cost-aware routing, and access control, but the current representation leans heavily on retrieval stack details and tool names.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The candidate can safely present governed enterprise AI workflow and LLM-gateway architecture with evaluation, access control, and production delivery.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Compress narrow retrieval/tool detail and surface governance, evaluation, reliability, and workflow-orchestration outcomes in summary, projects, and skills.
gap_analysis.citation: Underrepresented in current headline/summary/skills despite curated evidence of guardrails, access control, eval-driven gates, semantic caching, and production observability; category guidance prefers governed delivery over prompt-engineering or fine-tuning identity.

gap_analysis.why_it_matters: Privacy-aware delivery is optional but useful in this EEA category when supported narrowly and concretely.
gap_analysis.current_state: The curated store includes GDPR/TCF compliance delivery plus AI guardrails/access control, but the current representation does not connect them as a narrow governance-aware signal.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The candidate can safely claim privacy/compliance-aware delivery experience and delivery controls; formal ownership of AI governance or regulatory programs remains too strong.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Surface GDPR/TCF work once, paired with guardrails/access-control evidence, and avoid broader regulatory-program ownership language.
gap_analysis.citation: Supported by explicit GDPR/TCF compliance and guarded AI workflow controls, but currently absent from the representation layer; category guidance allows compliance terms only when directly evidenced and narrowly framed.

gap_analysis.why_it_matters: This market penalizes inflated management framing when the evidence is primarily player-coach architect scope.
gap_analysis.current_state: The current headline starts with 'Engineering Leader', and the curated store includes mentorship and hiring-process evidence, but direct-report ownership and performance-management accountability are not established as dominant facts.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Technical leadership, mentoring, architecture direction, and selective hiring participation are safe; formal people manager, director, or executive claims are not.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: De-emphasize manager/executive language in headline and summary and keep leadership wording tied to technical direction, mentoring, reviews, and stakeholder alignment.
gap_analysis.citation: Unsafe because the category is IC/player-coach heavy and only lightly rewards hiring/performance management; current evidence supports technical leadership better than formal management ownership.

gap_analysis.signal: Deep multi-cloud, model-training, or prompt-specialist framing
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The category explicitly favors applied architecture and governed delivery over inflated cloud breadth or model-science claims.
gap_analysis.current_state: There is evidence for AWS architecture, Azure/OpenAI endpoint routing inside Lantern, and enterprise AI workflows, but no evidence for broad multi-cloud enterprise ownership or training/fine-tuning pipelines.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Multi-provider LLM gateway and cloud-based platform work are safe; deep multi-cloud expert, fine-tuning, custom embedding training, RLHF, or prompt-specialist identity are not.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Keep cloud claims narrow and preserve explicit exclusions from do-not-claim files.
gap_analysis.citation: Unsafe because the evidence store explicitly excludes fine-tuning pipeline, custom embedding training, and RLHF, while category guidance warns against deep multi-cloud and prompt-engineering-centric framing without explicit proof.

headline_safe: Software Architect / Technical Lead for enterprise platforms and AI workflows
Enterprise AI workflow and platform architecture with governance, evaluation, and production delivery
Player-coach architect for modernization, observability, and stakeholder-aligned delivery
profile_safe: Translated business constraints, technical debt, and platform risk into architectural direction and delivery roadmaps.
Delivered governed AI workflow capabilities with guardrails, structured outputs, access control, retrieval evaluation, and LLM gateway patterns.
Combined brownfield modernization and prior greenfield platform work across event-driven, cloud-based systems.
experience_safe: Transformed a legacy AdTech platform into event-driven AWS microservices, reducing incidents by 75% and sustaining 3 years of zero downtime.
Served as platform lead for an internal enterprise AI workflow platform used by 2,000 users with 42 plugins, including evaluation harnesses, guardrail profiles, and per-silo access control.
Architected a 0→1 event-sourced CRM platform and supported market launch with scalable architecture, CI/CD automation, and requirements-shaping workshops.
Built observability, monitoring, and deployment patterns that improved release cadence, delivery predictability, and operational debugging.
leadership_safe: Mentored 10+ engineers on architecture and cloud patterns and helped grow 3 into lead positions.
Led stakeholder conversations on technical debt ROI, business metrics, and system risk under ambiguity.
Set architectural direction through DDD, bounded contexts, event-driven patterns, and north-star design.
unsafe_or_too_weak: Executive, director, head-of-function, or VP scope.
Deep multi-cloud expert across all major cloud providers.
Fine-tuning pipeline, custom embedding training, RLHF, or model-science claims.
Power Platform, Copilot Studio, Purview, or DLP specialist claims.
Formal AI governance or regulatory-program ownership beyond evidenced delivery controls and GDPR/TCF implementation.

well_represented_now: Brownfield modernization and platform reliability in the current role are already prominent.
Technical depth around retrieval, caching, and LLM-gateway mechanics is visible through projects and skills.
Seven.One remains correctly prioritized as the anchor role for seniority and impact.
underrepresented_now: Governed AI workflow architecture, guardrails, access control, and evaluation are not surfaced strongly enough in headline and summary.
Stakeholder translation, business-risk communication, and customer-facing requirement shaping are weaker in the representation than in the curated store.
Greenfield 0→1 architecture from Daypaio is materially underweighted for a category that values both greenfield and brownfield scope.
CI/CD, monitoring, and delivery-enablement mechanics are present in the store but not fully integrated into the architect narrative.
overstated_risk_now: The headline's 'Engineering Leader' wording risks drifting toward manager-heavy interpretation.
The skills list is tool-heavy and can read as implementation-first rather than architecture-first.
Representation is proxy-derived rather than a canonical hand-tuned master CV artifact.
representation_priorities: Replace generic leadership-first framing with architect/player-coach framing tied to enterprise AI workflow delivery.
Rebuild the summary around two pillars: Seven.One brownfield platform architecture plus Commander-4 governed AI delivery.
Promote Daypaio greenfield architecture and event-storming evidence into the visible supporting-role set.
Compress narrow retrieval/tool keywords and foreground governance, reliability, stakeholder translation, and production delivery vocabulary.
Keep privacy/compliance references narrow and evidenced; avoid turning them into broad governance-program claims.

curation_priorities.action: Promote current-role architect-title provenance from knowledge-base into canonical role metadata.
curation_priorities.why_now: This is the cleanest way to support an architecture-first title family without fabricating a new title, and it directly improves headline safety for this category.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: High: strengthens safe use of architect-focused positioning and reduces reliance on generic leadership framing.
curation_priorities.citation: Not yet curated from multiple upstream STAR records that consistently describe the current role with technical-architect wording.

curation_priorities.action: Promote Daypaio greenfield architecture, event-storming, and launch narrative into a stronger architect-facing evidence block.
curation_priorities.why_now: The category explicitly values greenfield plus brownfield scope, and Daypaio is the best proof that the candidate can architect from inception rather than only modernize existing systems.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: High: closes the greenfield gap and strengthens roadmap-translation evidence.
curation_priorities.citation: Supported by both upstream STAR #9 and curated Daypaio role bullets, but currently underweighted in the representation layer.

curation_priorities.action: Create a reusable governance-and-production evidence bundle for Commander-4 and related AI architecture work.
curation_priorities.why_now: The AI-architect fit is already real, but the evidence is fragmented across role bullets, project bullets, and skills, which weakens downstream reuse.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: High: makes governed AI workflow delivery reusable across category-specific CV generation.
curation_priorities.citation: Curated evidence already proves evaluation, guardrails, access control, semantic caching, and workflow/plugin architecture; it now needs tighter evidence packaging.

curation_priorities.action: Curate a narrow EEA-safe privacy/compliance linkage between GDPR/TCF delivery and AI guardrail/access-control evidence.
curation_priorities.why_now: This can add optional EEA relevance without crossing into unsafe regulatory-program ownership claims.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: Medium: adds EEA-relevant governance language while remaining evidence-bound.
curation_priorities.citation: Explicit compliance and delivery-control evidence exists, but it should be curated into a narrow, non-inflated governance signal.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Replace the current leadership-first headline with an architect/player-coach headline that surfaces enterprise AI workflow architecture and governed delivery.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The current headline is the biggest avoidable fit loss for this category.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: Current metadata underrepresents the strongest architecture and AI-delivery evidence while risking manager-heavy interpretation.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Rewrite the summary so it combines Seven.One modernization outcomes with current governed AI workflow platform delivery and stakeholder translation.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The summary currently shows only one half of the category fit.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: A category-fit summary needs architecture, governed AI delivery, and stakeholder translation together; the evidence exists but is not surfaced together.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Reorder Seven.One bullets so AI platform engineering, north-star architecture, observability, and CI/CD sit above narrower people/hiring bullets.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The category prefers architecture, delivery, governance, and reliability over hiring or team-building emphasis.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: This improves alignment with category signals that overweight architecture, delivery, governance, and stakeholder communication rather than formal management.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Refactor project descriptions so Commander-4 and Lantern read as architecture/governance/reliability proofs, not mainly retrieval-tool inventories.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The current project representation is technically impressive but too implementation-granular for this target category.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: The category values governed delivery, evaluation, reliability, and cost-aware decisions more than raw tool detail.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Rebuild the skills priority block around architecture, governance, delivery, reliability, and stakeholder vocabulary, with vendor/tool names secondary.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The current skills list is crowded with narrow technical terms and does not read as architecture-first.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: This category has no single dominant keyword; validated architecture outcomes should lead over keyword stuffing.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Bring Daypaio’s greenfield platform and event-storming work into the visible supporting-role narrative.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: It closes the only major scope imbalance in the current representation: strong brownfield, weaker visible greenfield.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: Greenfield proof is already curated and should be surfaced because the category repeatedly values both greenfield and brownfield scope.

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Anchor the category narrative in enterprise platform architecture and modernization outcomes.
Use governed AI workflow delivery as the main AI-specific differentiator.
Present leadership as player-coach technical direction, not as executive people management.
Elevate Daypaio greenfield architecture to complement Seven.One brownfield transformation.
Promote current-role technical-architect wording into canonical metadata before using stronger architect title families downstream.
Surface stakeholder translation and risk communication more aggressively in the master CV.
Use privacy/compliance experience only as narrow delivery proof, not as broad regulatory-program ownership.
Avoid executive, deep multi-cloud, fine-tuning, RLHF, and prompt-specialist claims.
